patterns.name: Deliberate Practice
patterns.summary: Deliberate practice is a structured, effortful activity specifically designed to improve performance in a particular domain, always with immediate feedback and focused attention on correcting weaknesses. It differs from mere experience or repetition: you push just beyond your current comfort zone with a clear improvement target in mind, guided by expert feedback. This pattern produces measurable skill gains that naive repetition cannot, by targeting the exact gap between current and desired performance.
patterns.intent: Build expert-level skill in a specific domain by repeatedly working at the edge of current ability with immediate, specific feedback.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a learner has cleared beginner level and needs to break through performance plateaus. Use when you can identify a specific, improvable sub-skill, a coach or feedback mechanism is available, and the learner can sustain high mental effort for focused sessions. Do not use for initial orientation or for skills where errors have immediate serious real-world consequences with no safe practice environment.

inputs.name: Specific skill target
inputs.description: A well-defined sub-skill or performance gap that can be isolated and practiced in a controlled setting, distinct from general experience.

inputs.name: Feedback mechanism
inputs.description: A coach, mentor, automated evaluator, or clear performance metric that gives immediate, accurate feedback on each practice attempt.

inputs.name: Motivated learner
inputs.description: A learner who understands that deliberate practice is effortful and not inherently enjoyable, and who is committed to improvement rather than mere performance.

inputs.name: Practice time allocation
inputs.description: Protected, recurring blocks of time dedicated solely to practice — distinct from productive work. Ericsson's research indicates elite performers sustain roughly four hours of deliberate practice per day.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A practitioner who has measurably improved the targeted sub-skill and can perform it closer to expert level than before the session.

outputs.name: Identified next weakness
outputs.description: A clear picture of the next performance gap to target — the output of each session is both improved skill and a refined practice agenda.

outputs.name: Performance record
outputs.description: A log of practice sessions, attempts, errors, and corrections that documents the improvement trajectory and informs coaching adjustments.

steps.name: Define the improvement target
steps.description: Identify one specific sub-skill to improve this session — not 'get better at coding' but 'reduce latency in spotting off-by-one bugs in loop conditions'. The target must be narrow enough to be measurable within a single session.

steps.name: Practice at the edge of ability
steps.description: Perform the targeted activity at a difficulty level just beyond current comfort. If every attempt succeeds easily, the task is not stretching the learner. If every attempt fails without insight, the stretch is too large. Aim for roughly 60–70% success rate — enough failure to generate signal, enough success to maintain motivation.

steps.name: Receive and process immediate feedback
steps.description: After each attempt or small cluster of attempts, receive feedback that precisely names what went wrong and why. The learner must actively process this — not just hear it, but adjust their mental model before the next attempt.

steps.name: Repeat with correction
steps.description: Re-attempt the same or a closely related task incorporating the correction. The loop of attempt–feedback–correction–reattempt is the core engine; a single round is not deliberate practice.

steps.name: Review and set next target
steps.description: At the end of the session, assess which sub-skill moved and which remains the binding constraint. Record the finding and define the improvement target for the next session. This closes the practice loop and prevents drift back into comfortable repetition.

principles: Practice must be effortful: activities that feel comfortable are maintenance, not improvement.
Feedback must be immediate and specific: delayed or vague feedback cannot correct the mental model in time to matter.
The improvement target must be narrow: broad intentions diffuse effort and make gains unmeasurable.
Volume of mere repetition does not substitute for quality of deliberate effort: ten hours of naive practice rarely beats one hour of deliberate practice.
patterns.threshold: The learner can name the specific sub-skill they improved, describe the errors they corrected, and identify the next weakness to address — without prompting from a coach.
patterns.masterpiece: A documented portfolio of practice sessions showing a measurable performance arc: baseline, targeted weaknesses, corrections applied, and current performance level. The portfolio demonstrates not just what was practiced but how the practitioner's self-knowledge of their own gaps evolved.

known_uses.name: Berlin Academy of Music violin study (Ericsson, 1993)
known_uses.quote: there is complete correspondence between the skill level of the groups and their average accumulation of practice time alone with the violin
known_uses.note: The founding empirical study; distinguished expert, good, and music-teacher groups entirely by accumulated deliberate practice, not natural talent.

known_uses.name: Chess grandmaster training programs
known_uses.note: Structured game analysis with coaches targeting specific positional weaknesses is the dominant model for developing titled players.

known_failure_modes.note: Learners mistake logging hours for deliberate practice; without targeted weakness selection and immediate feedback the activity produces familiarity, not skill gain.
Once performance reaches an acceptable level, the learner stops stretching and practice becomes performance maintenance rather than improvement.
Practicing without a feedback mechanism produces confident incompetence — the learner reinforces their own errors rather than correcting them.

sources.title: The Role of Deliberate Practice in the Acquisition of Expert Performance
sources.quote: the teacher designs practice activities that the individual can engage in between meetings with the teacher. We call these practice activities deliberate practice

sources.title: The Role of Deliberate Practice in the Acquisition of Expert Performance — key finding
sources.quote: individual differences, even among elite performers, are closely related to assessed amounts of deliberate practice

sources.title: Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise (Ericsson & Pool, 2016)
sources.quote: Deliberate practice nearly always involves building or modifying previously acquired skills by focusing on particular aspects of those skills and working to improve them specifically

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Define narrow improvement target] --> B[Practice at edge of ability]
  B --> C[Receive immediate specific feedback]
  C --> D{Corrected?}
  D -- No --> B
  D -- Yes --> E[Reattempt with correction]
  E --> F{Session goal met?}
  F -- No --> B
  F -- Yes --> G[Review: set next target]
  G --> H[More capable learner]

patterns.example_scenario: A junior software engineer can write working code but repeatedly introduces race conditions when reasoning about concurrent access. Her tech lead acts as coach. In session one, they define the target: spot race conditions in shared-mutable-state examples. The engineer is given five code snippets — she catches two, misses three. The tech lead gives immediate feedback on each miss: 'You looked at the variable declaration but not at who else writes it.' The engineer reattempts the three failures with this lens, catches two of three. They log: 'Thread-of-ownership tracing is the gap.' Session two targets that sub-skill specifically with eight new examples. After six sessions the engineer catches 90% of planted race conditions and can explain the ownership chain in each case. The portfolio shows progression from 40% to 90% detection, with a clear log of which mental models were corrected and when.

patterns.name: Coding Dojo
patterns.summary: A Coding Dojo is a recurring group session in which developers practice programming craft on a small, self-contained problem (a kata). The environment is safe: mistakes carry no production consequence. The session is explicitly about the how — the process, design choices, and clean-code habits — not about delivering a feature. Paired with code kata (a solo variant coined by Dave Thomas), the dojo is the social container that makes deliberate coding practice a team habit rather than a private discipline.
patterns.intent: Build programming craft and shared team norms through recurring, low-stakes group practice on self-contained problems.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when a development team has basic competence but inconsistent craft habits, or when the team is learning a new language, paradigm, or design pattern. Run as a regular recurring event (weekly or fortnightly) rather than a one-off workshop. Works best when the problem is deliberately small enough to be solved in one sitting, leaving time for discussion of process.

inputs.name: Kata problem
inputs.description: A small, self-contained programming problem with a clear success criterion — neither trivially easy nor so large it cannot be finished in the session. The problem should have multiple valid approaches so design discussion is possible.

inputs.name: Development environment
inputs.description: A shared screen or projector setup where one pair codes while others observe, or individual workstations for a mob/randori format. Tests are the verification mechanism.

inputs.name: Facilitated retrospective time
inputs.description: Fifteen to twenty minutes at the end of the session reserved for the group to discuss what they noticed about design, process, and craft — not just whether the problem was solved.

inputs.name: Regular schedule commitment
inputs.description: A standing time slot protected from feature work; a dojo that competes with sprint pressure will always lose.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: Developers who have practiced a specific technique or pattern in a no-consequence environment and can bring that muscle memory to production code.

outputs.name: Shared craft vocabulary
outputs.description: A team that has argued about design trade-offs together and arrived at shared language for concepts like cohesion, naming, and test granularity.

outputs.name: Completed kata solution
outputs.description: The code produced during the session — not a deliverable, but a concrete artifact for the retrospective discussion.

steps.name: Choose the kata
steps.description: Select or rotate responsibility for choosing a problem appropriate to the team's current learning edge. Classic examples include FizzBuzz (for TDD rhythm), Roman Numerals (for incremental design), and Game of Life (for emergent complexity). The facilitator describes the problem and clarifies the rules before coding starts.

steps.name: Code in pairs (prepared kata or randori)
steps.description: In a prepared-kata format, one person has solved the problem beforehand and walks the group through their solution step by step. In randori format, pairs take turns at the keyboard (typically 5–7 minutes each). The rest of the group observes silently and notes design decisions. The driver writes code; the observer guides; the audience watches without interrupting.

steps.name: Run tests continuously
steps.description: Tests are the ground truth for whether each step works. The session drives toward the smallest passing increment at each step. Test-driven development rhythm is intrinsic to the kata format.

steps.name: Hold a retrospective
steps.description: After the problem is solved or time runs out, the group discusses: What design decisions were made? What alternatives were available? What would they do differently? The facilitator ensures the discussion addresses the how and why, not just the what.

principles: The goal is learning, not delivery: finishing the kata matters less than the process discussion it generates.
Safety is the container: no production consequence means no ego defence — mistakes are data, not failures.
Repetition builds muscle memory: the same kata solved a second time with a constraint (no conditionals, no loops) reveals more than solving two different problems once each.
patterns.threshold: The team can explain not just how they solved the kata but why they made each design choice, and can articulate one craft principle they will carry into production work.
patterns.masterpiece: A team retrospective artifact — written notes, a shared wiki page, or a team charter entry — that records the craft principles the team agreed on across a series of dojo sessions. The masterpiece is collective, not individual.

known_uses.name: Paris Coding Dojo (Bossavit & Gaillot, 2004–present)
known_uses.note: First dojo session held 2004-12-13; the format spread globally and the Paris group grew to 1,600+ members.

known_uses.name: Codewars platform
known_uses.note: Industrialised the kata format at scale; millions of kata completions across hundreds of languages.

known_uses.name: Dave Thomas — codekata.com
known_uses.note: Thomas coined 'code kata' circa 1999, publishing 21 katas on codekata.com; the term entered common use through The Pragmatic Programmer.

known_failure_modes.note: When the dojo becomes about who finishes fastest, psychological safety collapses and the learning purpose is lost.
Dropping the discussion phase to fit more coding time removes the mechanism by which craft principles become shared — the dojo then produces individual practice, not team norms.
Rotating to a new problem every session prevents the depth that comes from re-solving the same kata under a constraint; novelty feels productive but does not build muscle memory.

sources.title: The Coder's Dojo — A Different Way to Teach and Learn Programming (XP 2005)
sources.quote: If I want to learn Judo, I will enroll at the nearest dojo, and show up for one hour every week for the next two years, at the end of which I may opt for a more assiduous course of study to progress in the art.

sources.title: Paris Dojo History — dojo-developpement-paris.github.io
sources.quote: First dojo session held 2004-12-13 with pair-programming groups; second session 2005-01-10

sources.title: Dave Thomas — Code Kata (Wikipedia summary of codekata.com, ~1999)
sources.quote: programmers write small snippets of code that they write in one sitting, sometimes repeatedly, often daily, in order to build muscle memory and practise their craft

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Choose kata problem] --> B[Code in pairs - randori or prepared]
  B --> C[Run tests continuously]
  C --> D{Kata complete or time up?}
  D -- No --> B
  D -- Yes --> E[Retrospective: design & craft discussion]
  E --> F[Shared craft vocabulary]
  F --> G[More capable learner / team]

patterns.example_scenario: A backend team of eight developers has inconsistent naming and test habits. They start a fortnightly dojo. Session one: the facilitator picks the Roman Numerals kata. Two developers sit at the keyboard in randori pairs, switching every six minutes. The rest observe silently, noting design choices on sticky notes. After 40 minutes they have a passing solution. The retrospective reveals three different opinions on when to extract a helper function. The team writes one shared rule: extract when the name would be shorter than the comment explaining what the code does. Six sessions later, code reviews move faster because the team shares a vocabulary for decisions they used to argue about from first principles every time.

patterns.name: Mastery Learning
patterns.summary: Mastery learning is an instructional strategy in which learners must demonstrate a pre-defined level of mastery of each unit before advancing to the next. Unlike time-based instruction, mastery learning treats aptitude as rate of learning rather than a ceiling. Given sufficient time and quality instruction, most learners can reach a high standard. The key mechanism is a cycle of instruction, formative assessment, corrective re-instruction, and re-assessment until mastery is achieved.
patterns.intent: Ensure most learners reach a high standard on each prerequisite unit before advancing, by treating time-to-mastery as the variable rather than the performance ceiling.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the subject matter is hierarchically structured so that each unit genuinely depends on prior mastery. Also use when learners have heterogeneous entry points and learning rates, and the program can flex the time allowed per unit. Avoid pushing all learners through on a fixed schedule. Less suitable for exploratory or creative domains without clear mastery criteria.

inputs.name: Learning objectives with mastery criteria
inputs.description: Each unit must have a clear, measurable mastery threshold — typically 80–90% on a formative assessment — that defines what 'mastered' means before the learner advances.

inputs.name: Formative assessment instrument
inputs.description: A quiz, checklist, or demonstration that quickly reveals whether the learner has reached the mastery threshold, distinct from a final summative exam.

inputs.name: Corrective instruction materials
inputs.description: Alternative explanations, worked examples, or practice problems for learners who do not pass the formative assessment on the first attempt. The correction must differ in approach from the initial instruction — not simply repeating the same content.

inputs.name: Flexible time allocation
inputs.description: A program design that allows faster learners to advance quickly while slower learners take longer on a unit — the critical structural departure from traditional instruction.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who has genuinely mastered each prerequisite unit before moving on, making advanced content accessible rather than confusing.

outputs.name: Mastery record
outputs.description: A per-learner record of which units are mastered, allowing instructors to see exactly where each individual is in the curriculum and what corrective instruction they received.

outputs.name: Reduced achievement gap
outputs.description: Across a cohort, mastery learning compresses the distribution of outcomes by pulling up the lower tail — learners who would traditionally fall behind instead reach mastery with more time and corrective instruction.

steps.name: Define mastery criteria for each unit
steps.description: Before instruction begins, define what mastery means for each unit: which skills or knowledge items must be demonstrated, at what accuracy level, and what the formative assessment will look like. The threshold is typically 80–90% on a criterion-referenced test.

steps.name: Deliver initial instruction
steps.description: Teach the unit using the primary method — lecture, worked examples, reading, interactive exercises. The instruction is designed for the typical learner; it is not yet individualized.

steps.name: Administer formative assessment
steps.description: After the instructional period, assess all learners against the mastery criterion. The assessment must be low-stakes and diagnostic — its purpose is to identify gaps, not assign grades. Learners who meet the threshold advance to the next unit.

steps.name: Provide corrective instruction for non-masters
steps.description: Learners who did not reach the mastery threshold receive alternative instruction targeting their specific gaps. This is not repetition of the original instruction. It uses different examples, media, or explanatory approaches to give the concept another angle.

steps.name: Re-assess and advance
steps.description: After corrective instruction, learners take a parallel formative assessment. Those who now meet the threshold advance. The cycle of correction and re-assessment continues until mastery is achieved. Learners who reached mastery earlier may work on enrichment or peer-teaching activities.

principles: Aptitude predicts rate of learning, not ceiling of achievement: most learners can reach a high standard given enough time and quality instruction.
Corrective instruction must differ from initial instruction: repeating the same explanation to a learner who did not understand it the first time does not help.
Formative assessment is a diagnostic tool, not a judgment: its purpose is to identify the gap so instruction can be adjusted, not to rank learners.
patterns.threshold: The learner achieves the pre-defined mastery criterion (typically 80–90%) on the formative assessment for every unit in the curriculum before receiving a completion credential.
patterns.masterpiece: A completed learning portfolio showing mastery records across all units, including which units required corrective instruction and how many assessment cycles were needed — evidence that the learner did not advance on partial understanding.

known_uses.name: Bloom's original mastery learning trials in Chicago schools (1968–1970s)
known_uses.quote: most students, perhaps over 90 percent, can master what teachers have to teach them
known_uses.note: Bloom's seminal trials showed that mastery learning lifted the lower performers substantially while not disadvantaging faster learners.

known_uses.name: Khan Academy — mastery-based progression
known_uses.note: Khan Academy's skill tree requires demonstrated mastery of each node before unlocking the next, directly implementing Bloom's model at scale.

known_failure_modes.note: Applying mastery criteria without flexing the time allowed defeats the model — learners who need more time are pushed forward unmastered or held back arbitrarily.
Corrective instruction that simply re-presents the original material does not address why the learner did not understand it; the cycle stalls without real correction.
Setting the mastery bar too low (60% or below) produces a false sense of completion and undermines the prerequisite logic that makes subsequent units accessible.

sources.title: Learning for Mastery — Benjamin S. Bloom (1968)
sources.quote: Most students (perhaps over 90 percent) can master what teachers have to teach them, and it is the task of instruction to find the means which will enable students to master the subject under consideration.

sources.title: Closing Achievement Gaps: Revisiting Benjamin S. Bloom's 'Learning for Mastery'
sources.quote: majority of students (more than 90 percent) would achieve successful and rewarding learning

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Define mastery criteria per unit] --> B[Deliver initial instruction]
  B --> C[Formative assessment]
  C --> D{Mastery threshold met?}
  D -- Yes --> E[Advance to next unit]
  D -- No --> F[Corrective instruction - different approach]
  F --> G[Re-assessment]
  G --> D
  E --> H{All units complete?}
  H -- No --> B
  H -- Yes --> I[More capable learner]

patterns.example_scenario: A company runs a six-unit AI fluency course for non-technical managers. Each unit ends with a ten-question formative quiz; the mastery threshold is 80%. In unit two (prompt engineering basics), eight of thirty learners score below 80%. Those eight receive an alternative set of worked examples using their own domain's vocabulary. The initial instruction used generic examples that did not land. Five pass the re-assessment immediately; three need a second corrective round with a coached practice exercise. All thirty advance to unit three having genuinely mastered the prerequisite. The facilitator records that unit two's generic examples are the binding failure mode to fix for the next cohort.

patterns.name: Spaced Repetition
patterns.summary: Spaced repetition exploits the forgetting curve: material reviewed just before it would be forgotten is remembered more durably than material reviewed when it is still fresh. Review sessions are scheduled at expanding intervals — short initially, then progressively longer as each item becomes well-retained. This achieves long-term retention with far less total study time than massed practice. The Leitner box system is the classic low-tech implementation; modern software (Anki, SuperMemo) automates the scheduling algorithm.
patterns.intent: Maximise long-term retention of a large item set by scheduling each review at the latest moment before forgetting, systematically expanding the interval as retention strengthens.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply whenever the goal is durable long-term recall of a large discrete item set — vocabulary, clinical drug interactions, protocol steps, API signatures, or any declarative knowledge base. Particularly valuable when the learner has limited daily study time and must retain material for months or years rather than for an exam in the next week. Not well suited to procedural skills or conceptual understanding that requires application rather than recall.

inputs.name: Item set with question-answer pairs
inputs.description: A collection of discrete facts, definitions, or associations structured as question-and-answer cards. Each item must be small enough to be retrieved in a single retrieval act.

inputs.name: Scheduling mechanism
inputs.description: Either a physical Leitner box set with labelled intervals, or spaced repetition software (Anki, SuperMemo, Duolingo) that implements an SM-2 or similar algorithm to compute the next review date for each item.

inputs.name: Daily practice commitment
inputs.description: A consistent short daily session (10–30 minutes) for reviews. Spaced repetition fails when sessions are missed because the items due pile up and the spacing calculus breaks down.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner with durable, retrievable knowledge of the full item set — able to recall items months or years after initial study with minimal refresher effort.

outputs.name: Optimised review queue
outputs.description: A dynamically scheduled queue that surfaces exactly the items that are at risk of being forgotten, neither wasting time on well-retained items nor letting fragile items decay past recall.

steps.name: Create or import an item set
steps.description: Break the knowledge domain into discrete question-answer pairs. Keep each card atomic — one question, one unambiguous answer. Complex concepts should be decomposed into multiple cards rather than crammed into one.

steps.name: Introduce new items in small batches
steps.description: Add new items to the active deck gradually — typically 10–20 per day — rather than all at once. Flooding the deck creates an unmanageable initial review burden.

steps.name: Review daily at the scheduled interval
steps.description: Each review session presents items due today based on the scheduling algorithm. For each item, attempt recall before revealing the answer. Judge your recall honestly — incorrect or uncertain responses reset the interval; correct responses extend it.

steps.name: Grade and reschedule
steps.description: After each response, grade it (again, hard, good, easy in Anki's model) and let the algorithm compute the next review date. Items answered correctly multiple times graduate to longer and longer intervals. Items frequently missed are reviewed more often.

steps.name: Maintain the habit until the item set is mature
steps.description: Continue daily reviews until all items in the deck have reached long intervals (weeks to months). At that point the maintenance load drops dramatically. A well-matured Anki deck of 2,000 cards requires only 10–15 minutes of review per day.
produces: mature item set with long intervals

principles: Review just before forgetting, not just after learning: the spacing effect means later review produces stronger retention than immediate review.
Retrieval is the mechanism, not re-reading: the act of pulling the answer from memory — even with effort — strengthens the trace more than passively reviewing the material.
Consistency beats intensity: a daily 15-minute session outperforms a weekly 2-hour session for long-term retention because the spaced intervals compound.
patterns.threshold: The learner can retrieve any item in the deck with high accuracy on a cold test administered at least one week after the last scheduled review session for that item.
patterns.masterpiece: A mature spaced repetition deck — demonstrable by showing that the items in it are scheduled weeks to months out, and that a sample cold test achieves above 90% recall — evidence that the intervals have been earned, not assumed.

known_uses.name: Anki — open-source SRS software
known_uses.note: Anki is the dominant open-source SRS platform; medical students in particular use pre-built shared decks covering pharmacology and anatomy.

known_uses.name: Duolingo spaced repetition engine
known_uses.note: Duolingo's 2016 ACL paper describes a trainable spaced repetition model calibrated on half-life regression from user response data.

known_uses.name: Sebastian Leitner — So lernt man Lernen (1972)
known_uses.quote: In Leitner's original method, published in his book So lernt man Lernen, the schedule of repetition was governed by the size of the partitions in the learning box.
known_uses.note: The original low-tech implementation using a physical five-box flashcard system.

known_failure_modes.note: Adding complex multi-part cards instead of atomic ones makes retrieval ambiguous and forces the algorithm to treat partial understanding as full recall.
Skipping days lets overdue items pile up into an unmanageable backlog; learners then avoid reviewing because the queue is overwhelming, creating a vicious cycle.
Revealing the answer before attempting retrieval defeats the testing effect that gives spaced repetition its power; recognition is not recall.

sources.title: Memory: A Contribution to Experimental Psychology — Hermann Ebbinghaus (1885, English trans. 1913)
sources.quote: Ebbinghaus published his hypothesis in 1885 after studying the memorisation of nonsense syllables; the forgetting curve shows that without reinforcement approximately 50% of information is forgotten within an hour

sources.title: So lernt man Lernen — Sebastian Leitner (1972)
sources.quote: In Leitner's original method, published in his book So lernt man Lernen, the schedule of repetition was governed by the size of the partitions in the learning box.

sources.title: A Trainable Spaced Repetition Model for Language Learning — Settles & Meeder, ACL 2016
sources.quote: spaced repetition is an evidence-based learning technique that uses expanding retrieval intervals to improve long-term retention

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Create atomic item deck] --> B[Introduce 10-20 new items/day]
  B --> C[Daily review session]
  C --> D[Attempt recall before seeing answer]
  D --> E{Recalled correctly?}
  E -- Yes --> F[Extend interval: good / easy]
  E -- No --> G[Reset interval: again / hard]
  F --> H[Item scheduled weeks out]
  G --> I[Item back in near-term queue]
  H --> J{All items mature?}
  I --> C
  J -- No --> C
  J -- Yes --> K[More capable learner - durable recall]

patterns.example_scenario: A developer advocate team must learn the API surface of a large cloud platform — over 400 service calls — well enough to answer questions live at conferences. The team lead creates an Anki deck from the official docs. Each card has a service name and a common use case on the front, and the correct API call with required parameters on the back. Team members add 20 new cards per day and review their due queue each morning before standup. After six weeks, each team member's deck is mature — all 400 items scheduled at 3-week-plus intervals. At the conference, a developer asks about a specific Lambda configuration parameter; the advocate retrieves the answer in seconds without looking it up. The retention outlasts the conference: three months later, a follow-up customer call covers the same territory with no need to re-study.

patterns.name: Retrieval Practice
patterns.summary: Retrieval practice uses the act of recalling information — not re-reading it — as the primary study method. The testing effect, demonstrated repeatedly in controlled studies, shows that actively retrieving a memory trace strengthens it far more than passively restudying the same material. This holds even when the retrieval attempt is difficult and partially incorrect. The counterintuitive finding is that testing is a powerful means of improving learning, not just assessing it.
patterns.intent: Strengthen long-term memory traces by repeatedly retrieving material from memory rather than restudying it, exploiting the testing effect.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply whenever a learner needs to retain and later use material — not just recognise it during an exam. Particularly effective for facts, concepts, procedures, and any knowledge that will be needed under time pressure or without reference materials. Use as the primary study method rather than a final review step. Less obviously applicable to purely procedural skills where physical practice (deliberate practice) is the mechanism.

inputs.name: Studied material
inputs.description: Content the learner has been exposed to at least once — readings, lectures, demonstrations. Retrieval practice requires something to retrieve; it is not an introduction method.

inputs.name: Retrieval instrument
inputs.description: A mechanism for attempting recall without looking at the source: blank-page free recall, practice questions, flashcards, or a peer who asks questions. The key is that the answer is not available until after the recall attempt.

inputs.name: Corrective feedback
inputs.description: Access to the correct answer after the retrieval attempt. Retrieval practice with feedback is substantially more effective than retrieval without; the correction strengthens the revised trace.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner with significantly more durable and accessible memory traces than re-reading produces, especially after a delay of days or weeks.

outputs.name: Gap map
outputs.description: A clear picture of which material the learner can and cannot yet retrieve — the errors during retrieval practice are diagnostic data, not just failures.

steps.name: Study the material once
steps.description: Read, watch, or attend the initial instruction. Resist the temptation to re-read immediately — one careful initial exposure is sufficient. The subsequent retrieval attempts are the actual learning.

steps.name: Close the source and attempt retrieval
steps.description: Without looking at the material, write down, say aloud, or otherwise produce everything you can retrieve about the topic. For flashcard-style retrieval, attempt the answer before flipping. The effort of retrieval — even when it is difficult — is what strengthens the trace.

steps.name: Check against the source and note errors
steps.description: Compare the retrieval output to the correct material. Mark what was wrong or missing. This step is not optional — retrieval without corrective feedback reinforces errors as readily as correct responses.

steps.name: Return to retrieved items after a delay
steps.description: After a gap of at least a day, retrieve the same material again. Items retrieved correctly the first time are likely retained. Items that were corrected need another retrieval cycle. Combine with spaced repetition scheduling for maximum effect.

principles: Testing is a learning tool, not just an assessment tool: the act of retrieval itself strengthens the memory trace, independent of whether feedback is given.
Difficulty is productive: the harder retrieval feels, the stronger the resulting trace — easy recognition does not produce the same benefit.
Errors are informative: failed retrieval attempts followed by correction produce stronger memories than correct retrievals, because the correction event is salient.
patterns.threshold: The learner achieves substantially higher accuracy on a delayed test (one week or more) than peers who spent the same time restudying — demonstrating that retrieval practice produced durable retention, not just short-term performance.
patterns.masterpiece: A completed practice test or free-recall document produced without reference to the source material, annotated with self-corrections — evidence that the learner is using retrieval as a learning mechanism rather than re-reading as a comfort mechanism.

known_uses.name: Roediger & Karpicke 2006 prose recall experiments
known_uses.quote: Testing is a powerful means of improving learning, not just assessing it.
known_uses.note: The canonical study: students who took practice tests retained significantly more after one week than students who spent the same time restudying.

known_uses.name: Medical licensing exam preparation — First Aid + Anki
known_uses.note: Medical students systematically use practice question banks (UWorld, Amboss) as retrieval practice instruments rather than re-reading textbooks; this is now the dominant USMLE preparation strategy.

known_failure_modes.note: Re-reading creates a sense of familiarity that learners mistake for mastery; retrieval practice breaks this illusion by forcing explicit production rather than recognition.
Attempting retrieval without checking the answer against the source reinforces errors; the correction step is not optional.
Doing all retrieval practice in a single intensive session rather than distributing it over multiple days reduces long-term retention; combine with spaced repetition for maximum effect.

sources.title: Test-Enhanced Learning: Taking Memory Tests Improves Long-Term Retention — Roediger & Karpicke, Psychological Science 2006
sources.quote: Taking a memory test not only assesses what one knows, but also enhances later retention, a phenomenon known as the testing effect.

sources.title: Roediger & Karpicke 2006 — abstract key finding
sources.quote: on the delayed tests, prior testing produced substantially greater retention than studying, even though repeated studying increased students' confidence in their ability to remember the material

sources.title: The Power of Testing Memory: Basic Research and Implications for Educational Practice — Roediger & Karpicke, Perspectives on Psychological Science 2006
sources.quote: Testing is a powerful means of improving learning, not just assessing it.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Study material once - initial encoding] --> B[Close source]
  B --> C[Attempt retrieval from memory]
  C --> D[Check against source]
  D --> E{Correct?}
  E -- Yes --> F[Schedule next retrieval with longer gap]
  E -- No --> G[Note error - correction strengthens trace]
  G --> H[Re-attempt after short interval]
  H --> C
  F --> I{All items consolidated?}
  I -- No --> J[Wait - spaced delay]
  J --> C
  I -- Yes --> K[More capable learner - durable memory]

patterns.example_scenario: A team of customer success managers must know a new product's pricing tiers and feature comparisons deeply enough to answer live prospect questions without a reference card. Rather than re-reading the pricing document repeatedly, each person practices retrieval. They read the document once, close it, and write out every tier, its price point, and its three key differentiating features from memory. Then they open the document, mark every error, and close it again. The next day they repeat the retrieval without reviewing. By day three, most managers can produce the full table correctly. A week later, on a call with a prospect, one manager answers a detailed tier comparison question in real time. The team that re-read the document reports more confidence but makes more errors on the same call.

patterns.name: Simulation-Based Training
patterns.summary: Simulation-based training puts learners into a realistic replica of a high-stakes environment where they practice consequential decisions and skills without risk to real patients, passengers, systems, or users. The simulator gives immediate feedback through the scenario's own logic — the patient's vital signs respond, the aircraft banks. A structured debriefing session afterward converts that experience into transferable learning. Originally proven in aviation, the method is now standard in medicine, nuclear operations, military training, and increasingly in AI agent testing.
patterns.intent: Build reliable performance in high-stakes, error-intolerant domains by practicing consequential decisions in a realistic but consequence-free environment.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when errors in real performance carry severe consequences — patient harm, safety failure, major financial or reputational loss. Also use when the situation is too rare to encounter naturally during training — emergency procedures, edge-case failures. Use too when the learner needs to build team coordination under stress before facing the real scenario. Requires investment in a realistic simulator or scenario environment; a low-fidelity simulation is better than none but captures less transfer.

inputs.name: Simulator or scenario environment
inputs.description: A realistic replica of the target environment — a manikin patient with programmable physiology, a flight simulator cockpit, a sandboxed production environment with injected faults, or a role-play scenario with trained confederates. Fidelity must be sufficient for the target skill to be meaningfully practiced.

inputs.name: Target scenarios
inputs.description: Specific, scripted situations the learner will encounter — an anaphylactic shock case, an engine-out procedure, a security incident response. Scenarios are chosen to cover high-stakes, low-frequency events the learner cannot safely practice on real systems.

inputs.name: Facilitator or debriefer
inputs.description: A trained instructor who controls the scenario (adjusting difficulty, injecting complications), observes performance, and leads the structured debrief. The debrief is where the learning is made explicit.

inputs.name: Debriefing protocol
inputs.description: A structured method for the post-scenario conversation — what happened, why decisions were made, what should change next time. Aviation uses Crew Resource Management debriefing; medicine uses the PEARLS or advocacy-inquiry method.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A practitioner who has made real decisions under simulated pressure and corrected their decision-making before those decisions matter in the real world.

outputs.name: Team coordination norms
outputs.description: For team scenarios, shared protocols for how the team will communicate, divide decisions, and handle errors under pressure — the coordination pattern that transfers to the real environment.

outputs.name: Performance record
outputs.description: A record of scenario runs, decisions made, errors encountered, and corrections discussed — used to track readiness and identify persistent gaps.

steps.name: Select and configure the scenario
steps.description: Choose a scenario that targets the specific skill or decision the learner needs to develop — not a generic 'practice session' but a scripted event with a known learning objective. Configure the simulator to the appropriate fidelity and initial conditions.

steps.name: Brief the learner
steps.description: Explain the scenario context, the role the learner will play, and the rules of the simulation. Establish psychological safety: mistakes in the simulator are expected and are the point. Do not reveal the scenario's intended complications.

steps.name: Run the scenario
steps.description: Execute the simulation. The facilitator observes and may inject complications according to the learner's decisions. The learner acts as they would in the real environment. The simulator responds to the learner's actions — this feedback is implicit, through the scenario's consequences.

steps.name: Debrief
steps.description: Lead a structured post-scenario discussion. Start with the learner's own assessment: 'What happened? What were you thinking?' Then reveal the facilitator's observations. Discuss the decision points, alternatives available, and what the learner would do differently. The debrief converts the lived experience into explicit learning.

steps.name: Re-run with variation or increased difficulty
steps.description: Where time allows, run the same or a closely related scenario again with the corrections applied. Immediate re-practice after debriefing consolidates the corrected approach before it can fade.

principles: Consequence-free failure is the learning mechanism: the simulator must be realistic enough that decisions feel real, but safe enough that errors are diagnostic rather than catastrophic.
The debrief is where the learning happens: the simulation creates the experience; the debrief makes it transferable. A simulation without structured debriefing is expensive experience, not training.
Target low-frequency, high-stakes events: simulation's comparative advantage over on-the-job training is access to scenarios too rare or too dangerous to practice on real systems.
patterns.threshold: The learner demonstrates correct decision-making and team coordination in the target scenario class without prompting from the facilitator, and can explain the reasoning behind each decision during the debrief.
patterns.masterpiece: A completed scenario run log with debrief notes showing the learner's self-assessment, the facilitator's observations, the corrective insights, and any re-run results — evidence that the simulation produced not just experience but explicit, corrected learning.

known_uses.name: Link Trainer — aviation (1930s)
known_uses.quote: Simulation training has its roots in the aviation industry, with the first flight simulators built in the 1930s.
known_uses.note: Edward Link's 'Blue Box' flight trainer was the first mass-produced flight simulator; the US military trained 500,000 pilots on it during World War II.

known_uses.name: Resusci Anne — CPR training (Laerdal, 1960)
known_uses.quote: The creation of the CPR technique led to the development of Resusci-Anne, a realistic simulator used to teach mouth-to-mouth ventilation by Laerdal Corporation
known_uses.note: The Resusci Anne manikin made CPR training replicable at scale and is still the global standard.

known_uses.name: Stanford CASE — Comprehensive Anesthesia Simulation Environment (Gaba, 1980s–90s)
known_uses.quote: At Stanford University, a group led by David Gaba developed the Comprehensive Anesthesia Simulation Environment (CASE), incorporating the aviation model of crew resource management for teamwork training in a realistic environment.
known_uses.note: Gaba's work brought aviation-style CRM simulation to medicine and is the founding case for medical simulation as a discipline.

known_failure_modes.note: Running scenarios without structured debriefing produces vivid experience but does not reliably convert it into transferable, corrected learning — the debrief is the mechanism, not an optional add-on.
Investing heavily in simulator realism while neglecting scenario design produces impressive equipment that targets the wrong learning objectives.

If learners fear being judged or penalised for errors in the simulator, they play it safe rather than practicing the difficult decisions — the consequence-free environment must be actively established and protected.

sources.title: Incorporation of Simulation in Graduate Medical Education: Historical Perspectives, Current Status, and Future Directions — PMC 2024
sources.quote: Simulation training has its roots in the aviation industry, with the first flight simulators built in the 1930s.

sources.title: Resusci-Anne origin — PMC 2024
sources.quote: The creation of the CPR technique led to the development of Resusci-Anne, a realistic simulator used to teach mouth-to-mouth ventilation by Laerdal Corporation under the leadership of Ausmund Laerdal.

sources.title: Stanford CASE system — PMC 2024
sources.quote: At Stanford University, a group led by David Gaba developed the Comprehensive Anesthesia Simulation Environment (CASE), incorporating the aviation model of crew resource management for teamwork training in a realistic environment.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Select & configure scenario] --> B[Brief learner - establish safety]
  B --> C[Run simulation]
  C --> D[Simulator responds to decisions]
  D --> E{Scenario complete or critical event?}
  E -- No --> C
  E -- Yes --> F[Structured debrief]
  F --> G[Explicit learning points & corrected models]
  G --> H{Re-run with variation?}
  H -- Yes --> C
  H -- No --> I[More capable learner]

patterns.example_scenario: A hospital is deploying a new medication override workflow in its EHR system. Before go-live, the pharmacy and nursing teams run three simulation scenarios in a sandboxed environment with injected edge cases. The edge cases are: a duplicate order alert, a weight-based dosing calculation near the maximum threshold, and a pharmacy-hold flag appearing mid-administration workflow. The facilitator observes silently during each scenario and notes every hesitation, wrong navigation path, and missed alert. In the 20-minute debrief, the teams surface three workflow assumptions that differ between pharmacy and nursing. They rewrite the shared mental model and run scenario two again. On go-live day, the first real edge case is handled correctly without escalation — the team had already made and corrected the mistake under simulation.

patterns.name: Microlearning
patterns.summary: Microlearning delivers a single, focused learning objective in a short, self-contained unit — typically under ten minutes. It can be completed in the flow of work rather than requiring dedicated training time. The canonical definition from Kapp and DeFelice (2019) centres on eliciting a specific, measurable outcome from a brief engagement. Microlearning works best as one tool in a broader learning architecture, reinforcing or extending prior instruction, and it pairs naturally with spaced repetition and retrieval practice for retention.
patterns.intent: Deliver one specific, measurable learning outcome in the shortest engagement sufficient to achieve it, accessible in the flow of work.
patterns.when_to_apply: Apply when the learner needs a specific capability update — a changed process step, a new regulation, a product feature — and cannot be taken offline for a full course. Use to reinforce prior training at spaced intervals or to deliver performance support at the moment of need. Use also to introduce a concept that will be developed later in a fuller session. Do not use as a substitute for foundational skill-building: microlearning cannot teach a complex skill from scratch, only update or reinforce one.

inputs.name: Single, scoped learning objective
inputs.description: One specific, measurable outcome the learner will be able to do after the unit — narrow enough to be meaningfully addressed in a short engagement. If the objective requires two unrelated capabilities, it is two microlearning units.

inputs.name: Media format matched to objective
inputs.description: The format — a 90-second explainer video, a two-question quiz, a single annotated diagram, a worked example with two steps — must be the minimum sufficient to demonstrate and practice the objective, not a compressed version of a longer course.

inputs.name: Delivery at the moment of need
inputs.description: Microlearning is most effective when available exactly when the learner encounters the need — embedded in a workflow tool, triggered by a task in a system, or sent ahead of a specific meeting or decision.

outputs.name: More capable learner
outputs.description: A learner who can perform the specific targeted action or make the targeted decision more accurately than before the unit.

outputs.name: Completed micro-unit
outputs.description: A short, reusable learning artifact — the microlearning unit itself — that can be re-surfaced as a spaced repetition prompt or shared across a team.

steps.name: Define the single learning objective
steps.description: Write one specific, measurable objective, such as: after completing this unit, the learner will be able to identify which of the three customer tiers qualifies for the new discount structure. If the objective cannot be stated in one sentence without conjunctions, split it.

steps.name: Choose the minimum-sufficient format
steps.description: Select the shortest format that can meaningfully demonstrate and practice the objective. A factual update may need only a 60-second video plus one confirmation question. A process step may need a three-panel annotated diagram plus a step-through simulation. Match format to objective, not to convention.

steps.name: Create the micro-unit
steps.description: Produce the content: one focused explanation or demonstration, followed by an active practice element (a question, a matching exercise, a checklist step). The practice element is what makes it learning, not just content delivery.

steps.name: Deliver at the moment of need
steps.description: Surface the unit when the learner is about to perform the relevant task. Good delivery points: in the tool they use for that task, before a meeting where the knowledge applies, or as a triggered prompt in a workflow. Just-in-time delivery maximises application and retention.

steps.name: Measure outcome and schedule follow-up
steps.description: Verify that the objective was met: did the learner answer correctly, complete the step, or apply the capability in the workflow? Schedule a follow-up micro-unit at a spaced interval to check retention and address any gap.

principles: One objective per unit: a microlearning unit that tries to cover multiple objectives covers none of them at the depth needed for the learner to act differently afterward.
Format follows objective: the length and medium of the unit should be determined by what the objective requires, not by a predetermined time box or content template.
Microlearning is a component, not a strategy: it amplifies and reinforces a broader learning architecture; it cannot replace foundational instruction for complex skills.
patterns.threshold: The learner completes the unit and correctly performs or predicts the targeted action on a real or simulated task within 24 hours of completing the unit — the micro-unit produced an observable change in behavior, not just a comprehension check.
patterns.masterpiece: A library of micro-units mapped to specific moments of need in a workflow, demonstrable by showing that a learner who encountered an edge case in production navigated it correctly because a triggered micro-unit surfaced the right guidance at the right time.

known_uses.name: Duolingo — daily lesson format
known_uses.note: Duolingo's five-minute daily lessons are the canonical consumer-facing microlearning implementation, combining microlearning with spaced repetition for retention.

known_uses.name: ATD — Microlearning: Short and Sweet (Kapp & DeFelice, 2019)
known_uses.quote: Microlearning is an instructional unit that provides a short engagement in an activity intentionally designed to elicit a specific outcome from the participant.
known_uses.note: The canonical practitioner definition that moved the field past arbitrary time-box definitions toward outcome-centred design.

known_failure_modes.note: Cutting a two-hour course into twelve ten-minute videos is not microlearning — it is a longer course in smaller pieces. True microlearning scopes each unit to a single objective, not a scheduled time slot.

A micro-unit that delivers explanation without any active element (a question, a simulation step, a checklist) is content consumption, not learning — it does not produce the behavioral change the objective specifies.

Using microlearning as the only learning intervention for a complex skill produces fragmented understanding without the foundational depth to apply isolated micro-facts coherently under real-world conditions.

sources.title: Microlearning: Short and Sweet — Kapp & DeFelice, ATD Press 2019
sources.quote: Microlearning is an instructional unit that provides a short engagement in an activity intentionally designed to elicit a specific outcome from the participant.

sources.title: Microlearning — Wikipedia: origins and definitions
sources.quote: Microlearning refers to a set of compact e-learning modules that are designed to reduce learner fatigue

sources.title: Microlearning in Health Professions Education: Scoping Review — PMC 2019
sources.quote: microlearning is considered to be a modern learning approach that attempts to address the increasing time constraints on learners by segmenting information into short, manageable pieces

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Define single learning objective] --> B[Choose minimum-sufficient format]
  B --> C[Create micro-unit: explanation + active element]
  C --> D[Deliver at moment of need]
  D --> E[Learner engages - performs active element]
  E --> F{Objective met?}
  F -- Yes --> G[Schedule spaced follow-up]
  F -- No --> H[Surface corrective micro-unit]
  H --> E
  G --> I[More capable learner]

patterns.example_scenario: A SaaS company rolls out a new contract approval workflow affecting 60 account executives. Rather than a mandatory 45-minute training session, the L&D team creates three micro-units. The first is a 90-second screen-capture video showing the three new approval steps, followed by a single scenario question. The second is a two-minute annotated diagram of the exception cases, followed by two decision questions. The third is a 60-second checklist for the most common edge case, delivered as an in-app tooltip triggered the first time each AE opens the approval screen. Completion takes under five minutes total. Two weeks later, a spaced follow-up micro-unit resurfaces the exception cases with two new scenario questions. Error rates on the new workflow drop 70% compared to the previous rollout, which used a recorded webinar that fewer than half the team watched to completion.
